title: Battenberg cake
subtitle: Marzipan-wrapped sponge in a pink and yellow chequerboard
excerpt: A pink and yellow almond sponge divided into four fingers, glued together with apricot jam, and wrapped in a sheet of marzipan. The chequerboard cross-section requires baking the batter in two separate halves of the same tin or in two small loaf tins. Intricate to assemble but straightforward to make.
sourceNotes: The Battenberg cake is an English confection traditionally associated with the 1884 marriage of Prince Louis of Battenberg to Princess Victoria of Hesse. The four-square chequerboard pattern and marzipan wrap are the defining features. The cake appears in British baking books from the late 19th century onward. The recipe here uses the standard all-in-one Victoria sponge base divided into plain and food-coloured halves.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponge a day ahead. Assemble and wrap on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. If baking both halves at once, rotate the tins halfway through.

recipeTools.notes: 20 cm square tin divided into two equal halves with a folded strip of foil, or two 450 g loaf tins

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of cutting two differently coloured sponge fingers into four equal strips and reassembling them in a two-by-two chequerboard pattern, glued with apricot jam. The assembled block is then enclosed in a sheet of rolled marzipan with the join running along the bottom. A sharp knife is essential for clean cuts.

content.text: is the technique the recipe teaches. The sponge itself is a plain Victoria base divided into two portions; one is tinted pink. Bake them separately. Cool completely before cutting: warm sponge will collapse under the knife. Use a ruler to cut the fingers to equal dimensions. Apricot jam is the glue; warm it with a splash of water so it is thin enough to brush.

attrs.prepNote: pink or red gel food colouring
ready-to-roll block or bought rolled sheet

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Line a 20 cm square tin with foil, creating a central dividing wall of double-folded foil standing 5 cm high. Grease both sections. Beat the softened butter and caster sugar until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time. Fold in the flour, ground almonds, baking powder, and vanilla. Divide the batter in two. Colour one half with pink food colouring. Spread each half into one section of the tin. Bake for 25 to 28 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool completely on a rack.

Trim the two sponge pieces to exactly equal rectangles (about 20 × 7 cm). Cut each in half lengthways to give four even fingers: two plain, two pink. Warm the apricot jam with a teaspoon of water and brush a thin layer. Stick one pink and one plain finger side by side, then place the remaining two on top in the opposite colour arrangement, brushing all contact surfaces with jam. Press together firmly.

Dust the work surface with icing sugar. Roll the marzipan to a rectangle large enough to wrap the assembled sponge block completely (about 30 × 20 cm). Brush the outside of the assembled block with jam. Set it at one edge of the marzipan, roll to enclose, and press the join firmly along the bottom. Trim the ends neatly. Crimp the top edges with a fork if desired.
